title: End of module: Trigonometry II, Identities, Compound Angles and Equations

questions.text: Given $\sin A = \dfrac{3}{5}$ with $A$ acute, find the exact value of $\cos(2A)$ as a decimal.
questions.explanation: Since $A$ is acute, $\cos A = \sqrt{1-(3/5)^2} = 4/5$. Then $\cos(2A) = 1 - 2\sin^2A = 1 - 2(9/25) = 1 - 18/25 = 7/25 = 0.28$. Check with the other form: $\cos(2A) = 2\cos^2A - 1 = 2(16/25) - 1 = 32/25 - 1 = 7/25$. The two forms agree.

questions.text: If $\tan A = 2$, find the exact value of $\tan(2A)$ as a decimal (3 s.f.).
Using the compound angle formula with $75° = 45° + 30°$, what is the exact value of $\cos(75°)$?
Express $5\sin x + 12\cos x$ in the form $R\sin(x+\alpha)$ with $R>0$. What is the exact value of $R$?
For $5\sin x + 12\cos x \equiv R\sin(x+\alpha)$ with $R>0$ and $0°<\alpha<90°$, find $\alpha$ in degrees to 1 decimal place.
Which of the following are solutions of $\cos x = -\dfrac12$ for $0° \le x \le 360°$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: $\cos x = -\tfrac12$ has principal value $x_1 = 120°$ (since $\cos^{-1}(-0.5)=120°$). Cosine is negative in quadrants 2 and 3, so the two solutions in $[0°,360°]$ are $x = 120°$ and $x = 360°-120° = 240°$. Checking: $\cos(120°) = -0.5$ ✓ and $\cos(240°) = -0.5$ ✓; the other options do not satisfy the equation.

questions.text: Which of the following are solutions of $\tan x = 1$ for $0° \le x \le 360°$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: $\tan x = 1$ has principal value $45°$, and $\tan$ has period $180°$, so the solutions in $[0°,360°]$ are $x=45°$ and $x=45°+180°=225°$. Checking: $\tan(45°)=1$ ✓ and $\tan(225°)=1$ ✓ (third quadrant, both sine and cosine negative so their ratio is positive); $135°$ and $315°$ give $\tan x=-1$, not $1$.

questions.text: Which of the following are solutions of $2\sin^2x - \sin x - 1 = 0$ for $0° \le x \le 360°$? Select all that apply.
questions.explanation: Factorising as a quadratic in $\sin x$: $(2\sin x+1)(\sin x - 1)=0$, so $\sin x = 1$ or $\sin x = -\tfrac12$. $\sin x=1$ gives $x=90°$ only. $\sin x=-\tfrac12$ has principal value $-30°$; since sine is negative in quadrants 3 and 4, the solutions are $180°+30°=210°$ and $360°-30°=330°$. So the full solution set in the interval is $\{90°, 210°, 330°\}$.

questions.text: Solve $\cos(2x) = \sin x$ for $0° \le x \le 360°$. Give the smallest positive solution in degrees.
questions.explanation: Using $\cos(2x)=1-2\sin^2x$: $1-2\sin^2x = \sin x \implies 2\sin^2x+\sin x-1=0 \implies (2\sin x-1)(\sin x+1)=0$, so $\sin x = \tfrac12$ or $\sin x=-1$. $\sin x=\tfrac12$ gives $x=30°$ or $x=150°$; $\sin x=-1$ gives $x=270°$. The smallest positive solution is $30°$ (full solution set: $\{30°,150°,270°\}$).

questions.text: Solve $3\sin x + 4\cos x = 2$ for $0° \le x \le 360°$. Give the smaller of the two solutions, in degrees to 1 decimal place.
questions.explanation: Write $3\sin x+4\cos x \equiv 5\sin(x+53.1°)$ (since $R=\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=5$ and $\alpha=\arctan(4/3)=53.1°$). Then $5\sin(x+53.1°)=2 \implies \sin(x+53.1°)=0.4$. Let $u=x+53.1°$, ranging over $[53.1°,413.1°]$. Solving $\sin u=0.4$: $u=23.6°$ (out of range), $u=156.4°$, $u=383.6°$, both in range. So $x = 156.4°-53.1°=103.3°$ or $x=383.6°-53.1°=330.4°$. The smaller solution is $103.3°$.

questions.text: What is the maximum value of $3\sin x + 4\cos x$, and at what value of $x$ (in the range $0°$ to $360°$) does it occur?
questions.explanation: Writing $3\sin x + 4\cos x \equiv 5\sin(x+53.1°)$, the maximum value of $5\sin(x+53.1°)$ is $5$ (the amplitude), occurring when $x+53.1° = 90°$, i.e. $x = 36.9°$. Checking: at $x=36.9°$, $3\sin(36.9°)+4\cos(36.9°) \approx 3(0.600)+4(0.800) = 1.8+3.2 = 5.0$ ✓.

questions.text: A triangle has sides $a=7$, $b=8$ and included angle $C=60°$. Use the cosine rule to find the length of side $c$. Give your answer correct to 3 significant figures.
questions.explanation: This reaches back to the cosine rule from Trigonometry I: $c^2=a^2+b^2-2ab\cos C = 49+64-2(7)(8)\cos60° = 113-112(0.5) = 113-56=57$, so $c=\sqrt{57}\approx7.55$ (3 s.f.). Spaced returns to earlier techniques like this are what make them stick.
